text:
Centre for Epilepsy

Neurosciences Division 

Clinic Date: 02 October 2025 

Dr Aisha Rahman 
Consultant Neurologist  
Centre for Epilepsy  
King’s College Hospital, Denmark Hill, London SE5 9RS  

Dear Dr Aisha Rahman, 

Samir Patel, DOB: 22-09-1992, Hospital No: K847251 NHS No. 1039284756  
Flat 12, 4 Brook Close, London, SW9 3AB  

Diagnosis: 

Recurrent unprovoked seizures; current classification not defined. 

Aetiology: 

No structural lesion identified on prior MRI (2023) and CT (2022). Routine EEG (2024) non-diagnostic. 

Other Medical Problems: 

Mild generalised anxiety symptoms (patient-reported), seasonal allergic rhinitis. 

Management Plan: 

- Medication: Continue Lamotrigine 150 mg twice daily, single brand supply. Reinforce consistent brand dispensation due to prior sensitivity to brand switches.
- Levels/Monitoring: Check trough Lamotrigine level and U&E/LFT in six weeks. 
- Adherence: Pharmacy supply has previously been variable; patient reports missed doses on several occasions in the past when stock was inconsistent. We have provided a written brand statement and a two-month prescription to reduce gaps. Patient to use a weekly pill organiser and set phone reminders. 
- Safety/Advice: Standard seizure first aid reviewed. Patient has been advised regarding medication concordance and to contact us promptly if supply issues recur so we can liaise with pharmacy.
- Follow-up: Review in four months or sooner if events increase. 

Interval History: 

Since June 2025, the patient describes brief events occurring on alternate days to most days in some weeks; on average, episodes are reported approximately every second day, with occasional short clusters around periods of stress or when a dose was delayed. Typical events last under one minute with loss of awareness described as "blanking" and post-event fatigue for 10–15 minutes. No clear focal onset features elicited. No injuries. Sleep deprivation and missed or delayed doses appear to precede a proportion of the events. There is no consistent diurnal pattern. 

Examination (today): 

General and neurological examinations were unremarkable. Weight stable; BP 122/76. 

Investigations Summary: 

- MRI brain (2023): No acute or chronic focal pathology.  
- CT head (2022): Normal.  
- Routine EEG (2024): Non-specific; no epileptiform discharges.  
- Lamotrigine level (May 2025): 2.5 mg/L at time of reported delayed doses (subtherapeutic). 

Discussion: 

Given the history of past variability in pharmacy supply and associated lapses in adherence, we have focused on securing a consistent single-brand Lamotrigine regimen and practical adherence measures. The frequency pattern described as occurring roughly every second day aligns with periods of suboptimal concordance and stress. No change to therapy today while we stabilise supply and reassess levels. If events persist despite verified adherence and stable levels, we will consider prolonged EEG monitoring and treatment adjustment at next review. 

Yours sincerely

entities.text: on average, episodes are reported approximately every second day

check__Seizure Frequency Number.analysis: The letter provides a clear current frequency in the Interval History: "on average, episodes are reported approximately every second day," with occasional short clusters. Following the rule to select the highest frequency, clusters could imply more frequent brief periods, but the letter does not quantify number per cluster; thus we cannot construct a valid cluster format. The explicit average "every second day" provides a numeric cadence: 1 episode per 2 days. There is no indication of seizure freedom. Therefore the normalized label is 1 per 2 day.
seizure_frequency_number: on average, episodes are reported approximately every second day
